story:
Ryan, a devoted single dad turned fiancé, is baking a birthday cake with his five-year-old son, Art, for Santiago, the man who has become their steady center. When Ryan suggests mixing the milk and flour while he cracks the egg, Art protests—he wants the egg job because “Papi taught me.” Ryan agrees, proud of Art’s growing independence, until the boy proudly smashes the egg on his own forehead. Tears and yolk follow. Ryan scoops him up, wipes his face, and carries him upstairs for a quick shower to avoid any chance of salmonella. Art perks up at the promise of the glittery raspberry shampoo with the mermaid on the bottle. Wrapped in a towel, he pulls on the shark onesie Santi gave him last Christmas.

Santi gets home from his shift as a firefighter, sings out a “Sweethearts, I’m home,” and catches Art before he can run down the stairs. He kisses Ryan hello, jokes that a coworker salted his coffee, and orders poke bowls when they realize it’s later than they thought. Ryan notes, not without pride, that Art is excited for rice and vegetables. They choose toppings—falafel for Art and Ryan, chicken for Santi; spinach, cucumber, tomatoes, corn, sweet potato, and seaweed for Art—then Santi sets up a blanket fort for their pre-birthday movie-night tradition. Art wants The Little Mermaid; Santi agrees and promises Star Wars when he’s older.

As they wait for dinner, Ryan thinks about how far they’ve come. He became a foster parent and adopted Art on his own, despite his brother’s doubts. He survived toddler chaos—the time he took a one-minute bathroom break and returned to find his two-year-old striped head to toe with marker—and the infamous lasagna flare-up in the oven that Art now gleefully describes as “Dad set the kitchen on fire.” He had sworn off dating until Art dated, but then Santi appeared, gentle and steadfast, winning both their hearts. Santi proposed months ago, and tomorrow he turns 36. Ryan and Art planned two surprises: a cinnamon coffee cake and a mug that says “Best Papi.” Art has been secretly calling him “papi” behind his back, waiting for the right moment.

After poke bowls and half the movie, Art falls asleep on Ryan’s lap. Ryan pauses the film precisely—Art is particular about resuming at the exact second. When Santi slips off to shower, Ryan eases Art onto a pillow in the fort, hurries to the kitchen, remixes the batter with a fresh egg, and gets the cake into the oven. By the time Santi comes back, Ryan has returned to the blanket fort as if he never left. Later, once the cake is baked and cooled enough, Ryan sneaks away again to frost it while Santi brushes his teeth, keeping the surprise intact. Ryan and Santi share a quiet kiss, both careful about boundaries. With a light, loving practicality, Ryan keeps intimacy for when Art’s out of the house; their cuddly insomniac often wanders into their bed. Far from resenting it, Santi cherishes being part of the late-night dogpile and dreams of raising a baby together someday. Ryan, who once planned to adopt an older child next, is open to starting over with a newborn because he knows how much that early time means to Santi.

When Art startles awake, he accuses Ryan of missing midnight. “I know you did,” he says sleepily, “because you went to the kitchen when Santi was in the shower.” Ryan—who sometimes “moves” the clock on holidays—plays along. They decorate the cake, light candles shaped like a 3 and a 6, and sing. Ryan cuts small slices to protect sleep. Then Art gives Santi the mug. “Don’t cry, papi,” he says, and Santi does anyway, laughing through tears. Later, when Art goes to brush his teeth, Santi presses close, whispering thank-you’s—grateful to belong to this little family Ryan built and chose to share.
